title: Giant critical response in a driven-dissipative quantum gas
authors: Ross C. Schofield, Daniel Lim, Himadri S. Dhar, Robert A. Nyman, Akshay K. Verma, Edmund Clarke, Jon Heffernan, Florian Mintert, Rupert F. Oulton
abstract: Systems close to a phase transition turn weak perturbations into large responses. At equilibrium, this amplification is closely linked to criticality: fluctuations grow, dynamics slow, and a common soft mode controls the response. Whether this correspondence survives in driven-dissipative quantum systems, sustained by continuous pumping and loss away from thermal equilibrium, remains an open question. Here we show experimentally that it does. In a room-temperature semiconductor photon Bose-Einstein condensate, the critical slowing of spontaneous intensity fluctuations and the amplification of weak pump perturbations are measured independently. Both peak at the same condensate population, $\bar{n}_c = 1250$, where the dimensionless slowing factor and susceptibility reach the same value, $\bar{n}_c/2 = 625$. A single weakly damped collective photon-reservoir mode governs both effects. This fluctuation-response correspondence in a finite open quantum gas establishes critical susceptibility as a measurable dynamical signature of condensation, with peak gain set by system size.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the link between fluctuations and response, typical of equilibrium phase transitions, holds true for a room-temperature semiconductor photon BEC. By measuring both spontaneous fluctuations and driven response, the authors show they are governed by the same collective mode. This discovery enables the use of photon condensates for highly sensitive, susceptibility-enhanced optical transduction.
main_problem: Investigating whether the fundamental correspondence between fluctuations and response (criticality) survives in driven-dissipative quantum systems away from thermal equilibrium.
main_result: The study experimentally demonstrates that a single weakly damped collective photon-reservoir mode governs both spontaneous intensity fluctuations and the driven response, with both peaking at the same critical condensate population.
method: The researchers used second-order intensity correlation measurements g(2)(tau) to access spontaneous fluctuation dynamics and pump-modulation spectroscopy to assess the amplification of weak perturbations.
model_or_system: A room-temperature semiconductor photon Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) in an open microcavity consisting of a planar DBR half-cavity, an InGaAs quantum well, and a concave dielectric mirror.
key_observables: Condensate population (n), fluctuation relaxation time (tau_slow), second-order intensity correlation function g(2)(tau), dimensionless slowing factor (Fs), and quasi-static susceptibility (chi).
important_parameters: Critical photon number (n_c approx 1250), cavity loss rate (kappa), spontaneous-emission coupling factor (beta), and dimensionless thermalization parameter (gamma_T).
main_assumptions: The analysis utilizes a linear-gain approximation within a Langevin framework and assumes the system is governed by a Landau laser-model-like free energy surface.
figures_summary: Figure 1 contains a schematic of the microcavity structure.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-equilibrium criticality, describes the experimental semiconductor microcavity platform, details the measurement of fluctuations and driven response, presents the comparison of the two via scaling laws, and concludes with implications for optical transduction.
why_interesting: It provides a rare experimental verification of the fluctuation-response relation in a non-equilibrium, driven-dissipative many-body system, establishing critical susceptibility as a measurable signature of condensation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental platform and signatures of critical slowing near condensation. (a) Schematic of the pla- nar–spherical semiconductor microcavity, comprising a single InGaAs quantum well embedded in the planar half-cavity. The cavity is pumped continuously at 785 nm and emits at 948 nm. (b) Sketch of the free energy surface versus order parameter (condensate electric field) and normalised excitation, for a Landau laser-model. This shows how weak restoring forces emerge at the critical point leading to diverging fluctuations and their lifetime. (c) Measured photon number as a function of normalized excitation, P/Pth, fitted with a rate-equation model, including: total measured photons...

FIG. 2. Critical slowing in intensity fluctuations. (a) Extracted intensity-fluctuation relaxation time τslow as a function of condensate occupation ¯n, showing pronounced slowing in a narrow mesoscopic window. Dashed line: Eq. 2. Inset: measured g(2)(0) versus ¯n (note that uncorrected uncorrelated background/multimode contributions can ren- der g(2)(0) non-monotonic; see Methods), overlaid with a phenomenological noise model. (b-c) Representative g(2)(τ) traces across the transition at ¯n ≃100 (b) and 600 (c) cor- responding to circled measurements in (a), illustrating the evolution from thermal bunching toward Poissonian statis- tics and the emergence of a long correlation time. Solid...

FIG. 3. Susceptibility enhancement near threshold. (a) Representative long-time g(2)(τ) trace measured near the critical region, showing oscillations induced by a weak nar- rowband intensity modulation of the pump at 2.1 MHz. In- set: pump-noise spectrum measured with a fast photodiode, showing the corresponding spectral feature. (b) Measured dy- namical gain G(Ω) at 2.1 MHz versus ¯n, showing large ampli- fication in a narrow mesoscopic window and rapid collapse be- yond. Dashed line: model prediction from Eqs. (4) and (5) for Ω= 2π × 2.1 MHz using parameters extracted from Fig. 2(a). Solid line: quasi-static susceptibility χ(¯n) = G(Ω→0).

FIG. 4. Common enhancement factor from sponta- neous and driven measurements. Dimensionless en- hancement factor extracted from fluctuation dynamics, Fs = (α + κ)τslow (blue), and quasi-static susceptibility, χ (red), inferred from the measured gain G(2π × 2.1 MHz) after cor- recting for the finite-frequency roll-off using Eq. (4). Both quantities peak at the same critical occupation, ¯nc ≈1.2×103, where χc ≃¯nc/2. Dashed line is Eq. (5), (α+κ)×Eq. 2 agrees.
